planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in fall in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; tumbleweed allium requires a dry summer dormancy period and is not suited to sites with summer irrigation or heavy, wet soils.

directions: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in fall in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; tumbleweed allium produces the largest and most dramatically structured flower head of all ornamental alliums — a massive spherical umbel up to 18–20 inches in diameter composed of dozens of star-shaped flowers on ray-like stalks of dramatically varying lengths, creating a loose, three-dimensional, snowflake-like burst that literally resembles a fireworks explosion. The flower is one of the most remarkable structural elements in the late-spring garden. After bloom, the dried seed head detaches from the stem and tumbles across the ground like a tumbleweed (hence the name), distributing seeds. The dried heads are superb in floral arrangements and last for months. Unusual and conversation-stopping in the garden.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; purple sensation allium naturalizes readily to form expanding clumps and self-sows freely in well-drained, undisturbed soils.

directions: Plant bulbs 4–5 inches deep in fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Purple Sensation is the most widely planted of all ornamental alliums, producing perfectly spherical, dense, globe-shaped heads of 100+ tiny, deep rich-violet star-shaped flowers on tall, upright stems in late spring — bridging the blooming gap between late tulips and early summer perennials with striking vertical structure. The globes measure 3–4 inches in diameter and the color is so saturated it appears almost electric. The allium family's typical onion fragrance is only noticeable when the foliage is disturbed. The dried seed heads remain attractive through summer. Excellent combined with catmint, salvia, and tall bearded iris in bold late-spring compositions. Bulbs multiply over years to form expanding clumps requiring no routine care.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, poor to average, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; blue globe onion self-seeds and naturalizes freely in well-drained, sunny conditions and often produces bulbils (small bulbs in the flower head) for easy propagation.

directions: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in fall in well-drained, poor to average, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; blue globe onion is a delicate and graceful allium from Siberia and Central Asia that produces perfectly round, cornflower-blue flower globes about 1–1.5 inches in diameter in late spring to early summer — the only ornamental allium with a true clear blue flower color (most others range from purple to violet). The slim, grass-like leaves are inconspicuous. It naturalizes freely by self-seeding and bulbil production in well-drained soils and is a charming component of rock gardens, gravel gardens, and wild-style meadow plantings where it can spread at will. Excellent for filling spaces between rocks and in gravel mulch.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees; English bluebells naturalize readily over years to form dense, sweeping drifts and should be planted where the foliage can die back undisturbed through spring.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade under deciduous trees; English bluebells are the iconic spring flower of British woodlands, producing drooping, one-sided racemes of deeply fragrant, nodding, narrow-petaled, violet-blue bell-shaped flowers on arching stems in mid-spring that form breathtaking sweeping drifts when naturalized under a deciduous canopy. They reliably self-seed and naturalize to create ever-expanding colonies that need no routine care. The foliage dies back by midsummer. Note: do not confuse with the Spanish bluebell (H. hispanica) which is more robust, upright, and less fragrant — Spanish bluebells hybridize with English bluebells and the hybrid is outcompeting the native in the UK. True H. non-scripta droops distinctly to one side and has a sweet, distinctive fragrance.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; spring snowflake is one of the earliest bulbs to bloom, often flowering before the last frosts, and naturalizes steadily to form increasingly beautiful drifts over time.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; spring snowflake blooms in very early spring — often alongside snowdrops and well before most bulbs — producing one to three white, drooping, bell-shaped flowers per stem with distinctive green (sometimes yellow) spots at the tip of each petal, creating a dainty, charming display. Unlike the taller summer snowflake (L. aestivum), L. vernum is dwarf and delicate, blooming for 2–3 weeks in late winter to early spring. It naturalizes steadily by bulb offsets in suitable moist, shaded conditions and is one of the finest companion bulbs for snowdrops, winter aconites, and early crocus under deciduous trees. All parts are mildly toxic if ingested.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant tubers (chufa nuts) 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; tigernut produces its best yields in loose, sandy soil that allows the tubers to develop freely and be harvested easily.

directions: Plant tubers 1–2 inches deep in moist, well-drained, loose, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade after last frost; tigernut (chufa, earth almond) is a cultivated sedge grown for its small, starchy, walnut-flavored underground tubers — the basis of the traditional Spanish drink horchata de chufa — that are rich in fiber, healthy fats, and natural sugars and may be eaten raw, roasted, or processed into milk, flour, or oil. Plants produce a dense clump of grass-like, bright-green leaves with triangular stems (a sedge, not a grass). The tubers multiply prolifically underground and are harvested after the foliage dies back in fall. Excellent as a forage crop for wildlife food plots (ducks and deer are attracted to tigernuts) or as a human snack crop. Distinct from the wild weed form (C. esculentus var. esculentus) which is an invasive nuisance; always plant only the cultivated var. sativus.
